structure.negative: Subject + be (am/is/are/was/were/been) + not + past participle + (by + agent)
structure.question: Be (am/is/are/was/were/been) + subject + past participle + (by + agent)?

explanation: The passive voice is used when the action is more important than the person or thing performing the action. It shifts the focus from the doer (agent) to the receiver of the action. The structure involves the auxiliary verb 'be' (in the appropriate tense) followed by the past participle of the main verb. The agent, if mentioned, is introduced by the preposition 'by'.
keyRules: Identify the object of the active sentence; it becomes the subject of the passive sentence.
Use the correct form of the verb 'be' according to the original tense of the active sentence.
Use the past participle of the main verb.
The agent (the doer of the action) can be omitted if it is unknown, obvious, or unimportant.
When transforming from active to passive, tense consistency is crucial.
azarNotes: Think of the passive voice as a way to 'de-emphasize' the doer. This is very common in formal writing and academic texts where objectivity is key.
Be careful with irregular past participles. Memorize them!
The 'by + agent' phrase is optional. If the agent is 'someone', 'people', 'they', or 'one', it's often omitted in the passive voice.

examples.passive: The meal is prepared by the chef.

examples.active: They are building a new bridge.
examples.passive: A new bridge is being built.

examples.active: The company had hired many employees.
examples.passive: Many employees had been hired by the company.

examples.active: You should complete this report by Friday.
examples.passive: This report should be completed by Friday.

commonMistakes: Using the wrong form of 'be' (e.g., 'The book are read' instead of 'The book is read').
Forgetting to use the past participle (e.g., 'The letter was write' instead of 'The letter was written').
Incorrectly forming the passive with continuous tenses (e.g., 'The house is being build' instead of 'The house is being built').
Overusing the passive voice, making sentences sound unnatural or indirect.
Confusing the subject and object when transforming from active to passive.
ieltsTip: In IELTS Writing Task 1 (Academic), the passive voice is essential for describing processes and changes objectively. For example, 'The raw materials are mixed...' or 'The product is then packaged...'. In Task 2, it can be used to present general truths or avoid stating the obvious agent. However, don't overuse it; a mix of active and passive makes your writing more dynamic.

quiz.question: Choose the correct passive form of: 'The students completed the assignment.'
options: The assignment completed by the students.
The assignment was completed by the students.
The assignment is completed by the students.
The assignment were completed by the students.
quiz.answer: The assignment was completed by the students.

quiz.question: Choose the correct passive form of: 'The government is building new roads.'
options: New roads are built by the government.
New roads are being built by the government.
New roads is being built by the government.
New roads have been built by the government.
quiz.answer: New roads are being built by the government.

quiz.question: Choose the correct passive form of: 'Someone has sent an email.'
options: An email is sent.
An email was sent.
An email has sent.
An email has been sent.

quiz.question: Choose the correct passive form of: 'The scientist will announce the results.'
options: The results will announce.
The results will be announced.
The results will announced.
The results is will be announced.

quiz.question: Choose the correct passive form of: 'You must follow these instructions.'
options: These instructions must follow.
These instructions must be followed.
These instructions must be following.
These instructions must follow by you.
